title: Hello at the Guesthouse
situation: Two new learners meet at a Seoul guesthouse.

title: Names and Countries
situation: A small language class checks names, countries, and occupations.

title: This, That, and Yes or No
situation: Learners point at objects on the classroom table.

title: Where Is It?
situation: A student looks for things before class starts.
targetVocab: 없다: is not; does not exist

title: Simple Classroom Actions
situation: A teacher gives slow classroom instructions.
grammarPointsIntroduced: Verb stem + 요 in polite speech

title: Native Numbers at the Table
situation: Friends count small items and people at a study table.

title: Phone Numbers and Prices
situation: A learner exchanges phone numbers and buys a cheap notebook.

title: Days and Time
situation: Two classmates coordinate class times.

title: Family Photos
situation: A learner shows a phone photo of family members.
targetVocab: 형: older brother of a male
오빠: older brother of a female
누나: older sister of a male
언니: older sister of a female

title: Food Likes
situation: Two classmates choose a simple lunch.

title: At a Cafe Counter
situation: A learner orders drinks politely at a cafe.

title: Daily Routine
situation: A learner describes an ordinary weekday.

title: Weather Check
situation: Friends decide what to wear before going out.

title: Question Words
situation: A learner asks basic questions around campus.

title: Shopping for Small Things
situation: A learner buys stationery and asks for quantities.
grammarPointsIntroduced: 이/가 subject marker in existence sentences

title: Around Town
situation: Friends ask where buildings are.

title: Transportation Choices
situation: A learner decides how to get to class.

title: Making Simple Plans
situation: Two friends make a low-pressure weekend plan.

title: Reviewing a Busy Day
situation: A learner gives a simple present-tense recap.

title: A0 Checkpoint: First Week in Seoul
situation: A learner uses early Korean to handle a first week.

title: Yesterday at the Cafe
situation: Friends talk about what they did yesterday.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is after a long time.' A very common greeting when you have not seen someone for a while.

title: I Can't Today
situation: A friend softly refuses a plan and explains why.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'my body is not good.' Natural spoken Korean for feeling sick or off; neutral plain form is 몸이 안 좋다.

title: Tomorrow's Plan
situation: Friends discuss a near-future plan.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: 꼭 means 'surely/without fail.' With -ㄹ게요 it sounds like a friendly promise based on the speaker's decision.

title: Because It's Raining
situation: A plan changes because of weather.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is fortunate.' Used when something turns out okay or a problem is avoided.

title: But I Have Class
situation: Friends negotiate plans with contrasts and background.
targetVocab: 가고 싶다: I want to go, but/and

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'time does not exist.' Natural for being busy; neutral form is 시간이 없다.

title: Polite Instructions at the Clinic
situation: A receptionist and patient use polite requests.
grammarPointsIntroduced: Please do X with verb stems

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'only a short moment.' Very common when asking someone to wait; a slightly more formal version is 잠시만요.

title: Trying Korean Food
situation: Friends encourage each other to try food.
targetVocab: 가 보다: have tried going; have been

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it fits the mouth.' Used for food that tastes good to you personally.

title: Can You Come?
situation: A small group checks abilities and availability.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'there is no problem.' Common reassurance; often pronounced smoothly as 문제 없어요.

title: Things We Have To Do
situation: Roommates plan errands before guests arrive.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'there is no mind/spirit.' Used when things are chaotic or you are too busy to think clearly.

title: What Are You Doing Now?
situation: Friends message each other in the middle of activities.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'what do you do?' In casual polite conversation it often means 'what are you doing now?'

title: Step by Step
situation: A learner explains the order of a simple routine.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'go and come back well.' Said to someone leaving and expected to return.

title: Before and After Class
situation: Classmates compare what they do around class time.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally from 깜빡하다, to blink/forget briefly. Common, casual-polite way to admit forgetting something.

title: Making an Appointment
situation: Two people schedule a study session.
targetVocab: 괜찮다: is it okay for you?

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'time comes out.' Used when your schedule opens up.

title: At the Pharmacy
situation: A sick learner asks for medicine.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.meaning: I feel like I'm coming down with a cold.
targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'there is a cold energy/sign.' Common way to describe early cold symptoms.

title: Housework Night
situation: Roommates divide chores naturally.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.meaning: It takes a lot of work.
targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'hands go a lot.' Used for tasks, food, or people that require lots of care.

title: Checking Into a Hotel
situation: A traveler handles a hotel check-in.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: A warm service phrase, literally 'rest comfortably.' Used by hosts/staff.

title: Hobbies These Days
situation: Friends talk about hobbies and frequency.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'I don't know that time is passing.' Used when something is so engaging you lose track of time.

title: Comparing Options
situation: Friends choose between restaurants and transport.
targetVocab: 비싼 편이다: is on the expensive side
맛있는 편이다: is on the tasty side

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is okay compared with thought.' Very common after trying something.

title: The Best Place Nearby
situation: Learners rank nearby places.
targetVocab: 제일 좋다: is best; like most

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: 말 그대로 means 'as the words say/literally.' Used for emphasis, not a proverb.

title: Because It's Close
situation: Friends explain recommendations with a practical reason.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is worth being so.' Used when a reason explains the situation.

title: What People Say
situation: A learner relays simple information from others.
targetVocab: 라고 하다: is called; says quote
이라고 하다: is called after consonant
좋다: they say it is good
비싸다: they say it is expensive
친구 말로는: according to my friend

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'there are many words.' Can mean gossip or buzz depending on context; here it means there is chatter.

title: Doing Things Is Fun
situation: Learners talk about activities as nouns.
targetVocab: 하다: doing; the act of doing

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is not a special thing.' Used to downplay a favor, mistake, or worry.

title: The Person I Met
situation: A learner describes people and things with relative clauses.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it remains in memory.' Used for memorable people, events, food, or scenes.

title: If It Rains
situation: Friends make conditional backup plans.
targetVocab: 시간이 있다: if there is time

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'lose the road/way.' Common expression for being lost while traveling or walking.

title: Going to Buy Tickets
situation: A learner explains intentions before moving.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'ate/took a heart.' Used when you decide firmly to do something.

title: Doing Two Things at Once
situation: Friends describe multitasking and simultaneous actions.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it comes right into the ears.' Used for clear explanations, catchy language, or audio that is easy to understand.

title: After Finishing
situation: A learner narrates completed steps.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'my heart is put down.' Used when anxiety drops after confirmation or completion.

title: It Seems Like Rain
situation: Friends make guesses softly.
targetVocab: 비가 오다: it seems to be raining

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.meaning: I get a feeling; I can sense it.
targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'a feeling comes.' Used when you intuit or sense something.

title: Is It Okay If I...?
situation: A guest asks permission politely.
targetVocab: 마음껏: as much as you like

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.meaning: Please eat as much as you like.
targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: 마음껏 means 'to your heart's content.' A warm invitation, less stiff than a literal permission statement.

title: You Must Not
situation: A guide explains prohibitions kindly.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally and figuratively 'must not cross the line.' Here used for a museum boundary line; also common for behavior limits.

title: Shall We...?
situation: Friends make and respond to suggestions.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'how shall we do?' Very common for deciding next steps together.

title: Could You Help Me?
situation: A learner asks for small favors in natural spoken Korean.
targetVocab: 천천히 말해 주다: please speak slowly

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'please look once.' A natural soft request for checking something, not only looking one time.

title: Have You Ever...?
situation: Friends compare life experiences.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it is like only a dream.' Used for wonderful or unreal-feeling experiences.

title: I'll Do It
situation: Friends make promises and volunteer actions.
targetVocab: 늦지 않다: so as not to be late

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'I will do it.' As a set response, it volunteers responsibility in a warm, practical way.

title: A Short Past Story
situation: A learner narrates a small travel problem.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'a big thing almost happened.' Used when something bad nearly happened but was avoided.

title: A Phone Call
situation: A learner handles a polite phone call and voicemail-like turns.
targetVocab: 통화 가능하다: are you available to talk?
소리가 작다: the sound is low
잘 안 들리다: I can't hear well

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'it isn't heard well.' The standard phone phrase when audio is unclear.

title: First Day at Work
situation: A beginner handles basic office and school-workplace language.
grammarPointsIntroduced: Formal workplace nouns with polite endings

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'no time to open eyes and nose.' Common vivid phrase for being swamped.

title: Dinner Invitation
situation: A learner accepts and responds to a home invitation.
targetVocab: 잘 먹겠습니다: thank you for the meal before eating
잘 먹었습니다: thank you for the meal after eating

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'I will eat well.' Said before eating to show gratitude to the cook/host or for the food.

title: Weekend Trip Review
situation: Friends coordinate a complete short trip.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.meaning: I feel light and excited to go.
targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'my footsteps are light.' Used when leaving happily or with relief.

title: A2 Checkpoint: My Korean Week
situation: A learner tells a fuller story about study, friends, errands, and plans.

targetIdiomsOrSetExpressions.explanation: Literally 'my mouth opened.' Used when language speaking ability begins to come out naturally.
